formula_note: Best = Karlshorst city-battle sum of 80,000 Red Army dead + 20,000 German soldiers dead. Low/high allow modest city-vs-broader-battle uncertainty while excluding ambiguous casualty-only sources.
uncertainty_note: High bound is deliberately only modestly above the Karlshorst total because broader Berlin-operation figures are not clean city-only military deaths.
quality_note: Strongest Berlin military-death estimate path is city-specific and side-separated by victim side.

formula_note: Best follows the tighter Karlshorst city-battle claim of 30,000 civilians. High carries the IWM 125,000 contextual estimate as a ceiling for broader Battle-of-Berlin interpretations.
uncertainty_note: Main uncertainty is scope: strict urban Berlin versus a wider 'Battle of Berlin' frame. The interval is intentionally wide rather than averaged mechanically.
quality_note: Best value is city-tight, but the high-end alternative is materially broader and less precise.

formula_note: No usable city-specific unknown-status death series identified.
uncertainty_note: Unknown-status deaths are excluded under a strict rule rather than silently folded into civilians or military.

formula_note: Episode length approximated as 0.4 month (1945-04-21 to 1945-05-02). Average directly engaged military exposure bracketed at roughly 300k / 400k / 625k personnel, anchored by ~85k defenders, a 464k city-assault attacker count, and a much broader 1.5m Soviet upper ceiling.
uncertainty_note: This is the largest denominator uncertainty in Berlin because attacker counts vary between city-only and broader assault definitions.
quality_note: Reasonably anchored by source counts, but still partly modeled because no single source gives a battle-average city-only force exposure.

formula_note: 0.4 month exposure multiplied by average civilians physically present in Berlin: low 2.8m, best 3.1m, high 3.5m.
uncertainty_note: The biggest issue is whether the population relevant to the death claims should be citywide Berlin or the tighter S-Bahn combat core. This estimate keeps the broader municipal civilian denominator but notes the mismatch.
quality_note: Good city population anchors exist at both early-April and immediate post-battle points.

formula_note: DR_v1 = (mil_deaths_direct / mil_person_months) / (civ_deaths_direct / civ_person_months). Low combines lower military death rate with higher civilian death rate; high combines higher military death rate with lower civilian death rate; best uses best/best/best/best.
uncertainty_note: Berlin's DR interval is driven mainly by episode-scope mismatch: city-tight 30,000 civilian deaths versus broader 125,000 Battle-of-Berlin civilian context, and by uncertainty over the average Soviet force directly engaged in the built-up city.
quality_note: Usable and source-grounded, but clearly sensitive to scope choices.

formula_note: Low = 19,000 German military dead from the local exhumation-style claim. High = 19,000 German + 18,000 Soviet military dead from the Radio-Moscow-reported city-battle context. Best = 28,000, giving full weight to the stronger 19,000 German claim and discounted weight to the weaker 18,000 Soviet claim.
uncertainty_note: The Soviet victim-side figure is the main source of uncertainty; accessible corroboration is weaker than for the German 19,000.
quality_note: One side-specific local claim is good enough to anchor the range, but the aggregate remains partly judgment-based.

formula_note: Best follows the local municipal-history snippet of roughly 2,300 civilian deaths. High expands to 5,000 to reflect contemporaneous reports of 'thousands' of bodies and possible undercount in the snippet-only source.
uncertainty_note: This estimate is highly sensitive to the inaccessible full methodology behind the 2,300 figure.
quality_note: The direct city-specific civilian number exists but was only accessible via snippet.

formula_note: No usable city-specific unknown-status death series identified.
uncertainty_note: Unknown-status deaths are excluded strictly rather than reallocated.
quality_note: Transparent omission is preferable to false precision.

formula_note: Episode length approximated as 0.267 month (1945-04-06 to 1945-04-13). Average directly engaged military strength is modeled at roughly 113k / 206k / 338k personnel, kept well below operation-wide ceilings and informed by the identified city-attack armies plus the depleted II SS Panzer Corps and ad hoc defenders.
uncertainty_note: This is the weakest component of the Vienna estimate because accessible sources identify formations better than they quantify city-only average headcount.
quality_note: Modeled from source-backed ceilings and city-specific qualitative order of battle, but not directly observed.

formula_note: 0.267 month exposure multiplied by average civilians physically present: low 1.31m, best 1.46m, high 1.61m. These averages are scenario-based reductions from the 1939 1.93m baseline using documented evacuation and migration losses.
uncertainty_note: The biggest issue is not whether Vienna shrank—it clearly did—but exactly how far the population had fallen by 6-13 April rather than by July 1945.
quality_note: Good displacement evidence exists, but battle-week average exposure still requires interpolation.

formula_note: DR_v1 = (mil_deaths_direct / mil_person_months) / (civ_deaths_direct / civ_person_months), with low/high formed from pessimistic and optimistic scenario combinations rather than mechanical averaging.
uncertainty_note: Vienna's DR range is very wide because both city-only military exposure and city-only Soviet military deaths are only partially observed in accessible sources.
quality_note: Informative but relatively fragile; best interpreted as an order-of-magnitude range rather than a precise point estimate.
